text: This is the case of a seventy-eight years old woman with an indeterminate middle lobe lesion, highly suspicious of primary lung cancer. After preoperative workup, we decided to offer this patient a uniportal middle lobe lobectomy.
The surgeon operates through a single three-centimeter incision in the fifth right intercostal space, beginning the dissection of the pulmonary hilum after confirming the resectability of the lesion.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary veins, noting that one vein originates from the middle lobe. The anatomy appears atypical, prompting careful dissection of all veins to determine which vessels are associated with the middle lobe.

The surgeon uses thoracoscopic instruments and an ultrasonic scalpel to complete the opening of the major fissure. A vascular anomaly is identified, with a branch from the lower lobe draining into the middle lobe vein.

In the drawing, we can better understand the unusual anatomy, with the aberrant branch emerging from the lower lobe and draining to the middle lobe vein. Also, the middle lobe vein and the vein that drains the superior part of the lower lobe have the same trunk.

The surgeon uses a right angle forceps to dissect the vein, preparing it for secure ligation with a vascular load, while carefully protecting the superior part of the vein. Subsequently, the ultrasonic scalpel is employed to ligate the aberrant vein.
The surgeon continues dissection through the major fissure, removing lymph node station XI located over the inferior lobe artery, which may aid in the subsequent dissection of the middle lobe artery.

The surgeon performs dissection between the middle lobe artery and its bronchus, preparing to complete the minor fissure. The superior lobe vein serves as a landmark for this fissure. A stapler with a thick tissue load is used to create the initial part of the minor fissure in the lung parenchyma.

The surgeon continues to liberate the middle lobe by dissecting in the minor fissure using hook electrocautery and endoscopic instruments. During the dissection, another vascular anomaly is encountered, with a branch from the middle lobe draining into the superior lobe vein. The ultrasonic scalpel is used to ligate these small branches.

The surgeon visually confirms that all middle lobe veins have been ligated, revealing the middle lobe artery and bronchus. The middle lobe bronchus is approached first and transected using a stapler. Thoracoscopic instruments are used to liberate the remaining connective tissues surrounding the middle lobe artery.

The surgeon divides the middle lobe artery using a vascular load stapler, ensuring adequate space for the instrument. The lung is repositioned to its anatomical location, and the minor fissure is completed with multiple purple loads from the stapler. Care is taken to avoid iatrogenic lesions near the superior lobe hilum during the stapling process.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy by opening the pleura inferiorly to the esophagus vein, then dissects the space beneath it to remove lymph nodes from stations four and two. An anterior lymphadenectomy is also conducted at station seven.

The final surgical view shows the pulmonary hilum with two inferior lobe veins and the superior lobe vein clearly visible. An intercostal nerve blockade is performed, followed by the insertion of a twenty-four French chest tube.
